scenario: You have a strong-LOOKING hand (overpair / top pair top kicker / two pair). Stacks are 100bb+ and a lot of money is about to go in.
question: Before stacking off 100bb+, what is the ONE question to ask?
options: Does my hand beat their value range, or just look strong?
Is my hand better than average?
Do I have at least top pair?
Am I getting a decent price?
explanation: This is THE discipline that fixes your biggest leak. Strong-LOOKING hands (overpairs, TPTK, two pair) are not the same as hands that beat the range committing 100bb. When stacks go in deep, villain's range narrows to the nuts-ish: sets, better two pair, dominating aces, AA/KK. Ask: does my hand beat that VALUE range? If it only beats bluffs and worse one-pair hands, it's a fold — no matter how strong it looks.

scenario: A loose-aggressive villain (47% VPIP, 20% 3-bet) has been 'pushing a lot'. He 5-bet jams 100bb+ over your 4-bet.
question: Villain is loose/aggressive overall. Does that mean his 100bb 5-bet-JAM range is wide?
options: No — even a maniac's 100bb jam is mostly QQ+/AK
Yes — he's loose, so call him wide
Yes — he could have anything here
Only if he happens to be on tilt
explanation: This is the exact error from your AJo hand. A player can be 47% VPIP / 20% 3-bet and STILL only 5-bet-jam 100bb with QQ+, AK. Loose players are wide INTO pots and on early streets — but their full-stack commitment range collapses to a strong core. You correctly read he was aggressive; the mistake was applying 'he's wide' to a 100bb jam, where it doesn't hold. Wide preflop ≠ wide all-in.

scenario: You have AJo in the SB, 100bb+ deep. MP opens to 2.5bb, you 3-bet to 9bb, MP 4-bets to 23bb, you 5-bet to 50bb, MP jams 100bb+ all-in.
question: You 5-bet AJo and faced a 100bb jam. Call or fold?
options: Fold — AJo is dominated by the jam range
Call — you're priced in
Call — he's aggressive
Call — AJ is strong
explanation: From your own play (you called and lost 105bb; villain had AK — exactly what you told yourself he didn't have). AJo is one of the worst hands to stack off 100bb because it's DOMINATED by the jamming range: AK and AQ dominate it, AA has it well beaten, you're only flipping vs QQ-JJ. AJo wants to 3-bet and FOLD to the 4-bet, or 3-bet/call once — never build a 5-bet stack-off pot. The aggression read was fine; the hand choice was the leak.

scenario: You have KK in the SB. Flop J-5-4, you bet (called). Turn 6: you check, villain bets, you check-raise big, villain re-raises all-in over you.
question: You have KK (overpair) on J-5-4-6. Villain re-raises all-in over your turn check-raise. Action?
options: Fold — the re-raise reps a set; KK is one pair
Call — KK is too strong to fold
It's a cooler, call it
Re-raise
explanation: From your own play (you called and lost 117bb; villain had a set of jacks). KK LOOKS strong, but the ACTION redefines it: a re-raise over your turn check-raise is sets and two pair — hands that are well ahead of one pair. An overpair is still ONE PAIR. When the betting screams a set, even KK folds. This is the leak's signature: a strong-looking hand committed against a range that beats it.

scenario: You have AQs on the BTN. Flop K-4-8, you c-bet (called). Turn 4: villain leads big into you.
question: You have AQ on a K-high board — you don't even have a pair. Villain bets into you on the turn. Commit?
options: Fold — ace-high commits to nothing here
Call — overcards
Raise — represent the king
Call — gutshot equity
explanation: From your own play (you raised/committed and lost 105bb; villain had QQ). AQ on K-4-8 is just ace-high — no pair. Stacking off ace-high vs a villain leading into you is drawing nearly dead to a pair. Same family as the AJ and AK hands: committing a big pot with a hand that doesn't beat the value range. Ace-high commits to nothing.

scenario: You have AKo on the BTN. Flop 8-7-6 two-tone (very wet), you c-bet 13bb, villain check-raises.
question: AK-high on 8-7-6 two-tone, villain check-raises. Commit?
options: Fold — ace-high, no pair, no draw vs a raise
Call — two overcards
Re-raise as a bluff
Call to hit
explanation: From your own play (you committed and lost 128bb; villain had AA). On 8-7-6 two-tone, a check-raise is sets, two pair, straights, big draws, and overpairs. Ace-high has no pair and no clean draw. C-betting was fine; committing to the check-raise with air is the leak. Fold and keep your stack.

scenario: You have TPTK (top pair top kicker) 100bb deep. Villain check-raises the flop and jams the turn on a dry board.
question: TPTK, 100bb deep, villain check-raise then turn jam on a dry board. Commit?
options: Fold — dry-board check-raise/jam is value; TPTK is one pair
Call — TPTK is the top of my range
Call — he could be bluffing
Raise
explanation: On a dry board there are no draws, so villain has no reason to fast-play unless he has it. A check-raise-then-jam line on a dry board is the nuts-ish: sets, two pair. TPTK is one pair — strong-looking, second-best here. Fold. Dry board + huge aggression = value, and one pair isn't enough to stack off.

scenario: You open AQo, a tight player 3-bets, you call. Flop comes Q-high. You bet, they raise big, then jam the turn.
question: Top pair (Q) with A kicker vs a tight 3-bettor who raises flop and jams turn. Commit?
options: Lean fold — a tight player's jam beats top pair
Snap call, TPTK is huge
Always call top pair
Re-raise
explanation: Against a TIGHT 3-bettor specifically, top pair good kicker is in bad shape when they commit: their value range is overpairs (AA/KK) that beat you, QQ (a set), AQ (a chop), and sets. You beat almost nothing that stacks off. Tight range + big commitment = fold your one pair. Read the OPPONENT'S range, not just your hand's strength.

scenario: You've mentally decided you're near the end of your session. A marginal spot comes up.
question: Once you've decided you're about to finish, how should you play?
options: Finish now, or only play tighter
Loosen up for one last big pot
Play exactly as before
Gamble more since you're nearly done
explanation: Once you've decided you're near the end, only two choices are sound regardless of your stack: quit now, or play tighter. Deciding to finish quietly drops your focus and creates an urge to 'finish on action' — so marginal spots get played looser. The 'disaster that always happens when I say I'll quit' is YOU loosening up, not variance. A leaving player folds everything marginal — or just leaves.

scenario: You have 66 on a J-J-6 flop — sixes full of jacks (a full house). Villain jams big over your bet.
question: You have a full house (low boat) on a PAIRED board and villain jams. Always an easy commit?
options: Pause — a low full house isn't the nuts on a paired board: any Jx makes a bigger boat (jacks full), JJ is quads. Read the board and range.
Snap it in, a full house is the nuts
Fold the full house
Full houses never lose
explanation: A set on a paired board is automatically a FULL HOUSE — but not always a winning one. Here 66 on J-J-6 is sixes full of jacks, which loses to any Jx (jacks full of sixes) and to JJ (quads). The discipline: a LOW full house on a paired board looks like a monster but can be second-best. Usually you commit; the skill is recognizing the paired-board spots where a bigger boat is in villain's jamming range. Always ask what beats you on THIS board.

scenario: Reviewing your biggest losses: AJ vs AK, KK vs a set, AQ vs QQ, AK vs AA, KQ vs AA.
question: What do all these expensive losses have in common?
options: A strong-looking hand committed against a better range
Pure bad luck / variance
Bad cards
Playing too tight
explanation: Every one is the SAME leak: getting a strong-but-second-best hand all-in against a value range that dominates it. It's not variance (you weren't ahead) and not bad cards — it's one fixable discipline error, concentrated in the biggest pots where it costs the most. Fix the commitment decision and the bulk of your big losses disappear. This is the single most valuable fix in your game right now.
